coredata.dc:title: Triarylamine-based polyamide containing adamantane group as Fe3+ probe and electrochromic smart devices
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dongxu
Wang, Xiaohong
Dai, Juguo
Liu, Hao
Xu, Yiting
Yuan, Conghui
Chen, Guorong
Dai, Lizong
coredata.dc:description: A new triarylamine-based polyamide was synthesized from 1,4-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid and diamine monomer containing adamantane group (PA-CA), which exhibits high fluorescence quantum up to 46.1% and excellent electrochromic behaviors. The photoluminescence of PA-CA was quenched by coordination and internal filtration effect to realize Fe3+ response. Neutral colorless PA-CA film was converted to the oxidized blue in the electrochemical oxidation process and the maximum transmittance reached 80%, and the transmittance still exceeded 55% after 10,000 s. A Sandwich structure electrochromic device with PA-CA as the active layer was constructed with a simple strategy and different contents were displayed through patterned customization. Electrofluorochromic performance was regulated and decoding information was customized by applying voltage changes of the light and dark for PA-CA film. The applications of multifunctional integrated PA-CA responding to stimulus have broad prospects in the field of Fe3+ fluorescence sensors, photodetectors and multicolor electrochromic display devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyamide
Electrochromic
Electrofluorochromic
Sensor
Smart devices

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 1H spectrum, (b) 13C spectrum, (c) HSQC spectrum of 1AATAA. (d) Infrared spectra of 1ANTAA, 1AATAA and PA-CA.
(a) XRD patterns of PA-CA. (b) TGA curves of PA-CA in N2.

(a) The UV–vis and PL emission spectra of PA-CA. Inset: photographs of solution, film, blank hydrogel and hydrogel which added PA-C from left to right, respectively. (b) The CIE1931 coordinates (X,Y) of PA-CA solution, the solid film, and the hydrogel which added PA-CA. (c) The mechanism of PA-CA film is colorless and strong fluorescence emission. (d) The transmittance of PA-CA film.

(a) PL response of PA-CA to Fe3+ concentration (0–30 μg/mL). (b) Correlation between Fe3 concentration and (I0/I)− 1. (c) UV–vis response of PA-CA to Fe3+ concentration. (d) Correlation between Fe3+ concentration and absorbance.

(a) Comparison of PL intensity of PA-CA after adding different metal ions and Fe3+. (b) Fluorescence comparison images of PA-CA after adding different metal ions and Fe3+ (above: natural light, below: UV light). (c) PL spectra of multiple ions coexisting with Fe3+. (d) Fluorescence comparison images of multiple ions coexisting with Fe3+ (above: natural light, below: UV light).

(a)The excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of PA-CA and the absorption spectrum of Fe3+. (b) The 1HNMR and infrared spectra of PA-CA before and after the addition of Fe3+ (c)The influence of other metal ions on PA-CA fluorescence. (d) The possible coordination mechanism of Fe3+ and PA-CA.

(a) The CV curve, (b) the DPV curve, (c)and the CV curves at different scan rates of PA-CA on ITO/glass electrode in 0.2 M TBAP/ACN solution. (d) The relationship between i
p and v
1/2 .

(a) Electronic absorption spectrum of PA-CA ﬁlm from 0.0 to 1.0 V. (b) The transmittance of PA-CA changes from neutral to oxidized state and the CIE1931 coordinate (X, Y). (c) Dynamic changes of the transmittance and current. (d) Response time for PA-CA.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of PA-CA at different potentials from 0 to 1.0 V. (b) The mechanism of information transmission through regulating PL intensity.

(a) The assembly and working principle, (b) the CV curves for 300 cycles, (c) and dynamic changes of the transmittance (d) the transmittance in neutral state and oxidation state of electrochromic device.
Photophysical properties of solution and film of the PA-CA.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: measured in NMP solution (1 ×10−5 mol L−1).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c

head.ce:title: Triarylamine-based polyamide containing adamantane group as Fe3+ probe and electrochromic smart devices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new triarylamine-based polyamide was synthesized from 1,4-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid and diamine monomer containing adamantane group (PA-CA), which exhibits high fluorescence quantum up to 46.1% and excellent electrochromic behaviors. The photoluminescence of PA-CA was quenched by coordination and internal filtration effect to realize Fe3+ response. Neutral colorless PA-CA film was converted to the oxidized blue in the electrochemical oxidation process and the maximum transmittance reached 80%, and the transmittance still exceeded 55% after 10,000 s. A Sandwich structure electrochromic device with PA-CA as the active layer was constructed with a simple strategy and different contents were displayed through patterned customization. Electrofluorochromic performance was regulated and decoding information was customized by applying voltage changes of the light and dark for PA-CA film. The applications of multifunctional integrated PA-CA responding to stimulus have broad prospects in the field of Fe3+ fluorescence sensors, photodetectors and multicolor electrochromic display devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic electrochromic (EC) materials with reversible changes in transmittance or absorbance in the process of electrochemical oxidation and reduction, which have attracted more attention in the field of photoelectric displays [1,2], dimming glass [3,4], optoelectronic memory [5,6], military camouflage [7], sensors [8], due to their low starting voltage, fast response, and fantastic color change [9–11]. Low power consumption electrochromic can display different contents according to requirements and has been used as a real-time visual monitor display platform of measurement data [12]. However, it is still challenging to obtain EC materials that are transparent under natural light, with high optical transmittance contrast, fast redox switching, and long-term cycle stability under external electric field stimulation. Liou and co-workers have developed a series of small molecule EC and electrofluorochromic (EFC) devices with triarylamine (TAA) groups, which has obtained stable and fast switching responses [13,14]. Small molecular compounds are easy to dissolve and facilitate large-area solution processing. On the contrary, their poor thermal stability is difficult to meet the processing under high temperature and harsh conditions. Although conjugated polymer EC materials with good thermal stability such as polyaniline and polypyrene can meet the processing scenarios at high temperatures, their dark color, poor solubility and difficulty in forming independent films limit their large-area processing [15,16].

Aromatic polyamides (PAs) were usually used as engineering plastics [17] with good processability, thermal stability and, mechanical properties. Furthermore, the introduction of electroactive TAA groups into the structure of PAs can realize the construction of photoactive PAs used in the photoelectric field [18–20]. The properties of TAA-based PAs can be adjusted by designing the monomer structure. Zhou and co-workers used pyrene and spirofluorene groups as benzene bridges to link two diphenylamine units to prepare TAA-based PAs, obtaining optoelectronically active PAs with dual functions of EC and EFC properties [21]. The adamantane groups have been reported to be commonly used to increase the solubility and thermal stability of polymers [22]. Likewise, the adamantane group with a large steric hindrance effect can suppress the geometric deformation of the excited state, which helps to suppress the Stokes shift and the non-radiative decay process, and has been reported to be used in the light-emitting layer of high-performance organic light-emitting diode devices [23]. Accordingly, adamantane groups are excellent candidates for constructing bifunctional EC materials. According to the EC and EFC characteristics, controlling the color change or the change of the fluorescence intensity of the materials by changing the voltage is a reliable encryption method to realize the transmission of information. PAs with fluorescence also have broader application prospect in the field of fluorescence sensing. Ferric ions (Fe3+) play an important role in the environment and the human body [24]. The development of sensitive Fe3+ detection and sensing methods is of great significance to the environment and human health. However, the commercial application of these probes is limited by the complex methods, low response rate and high cost [25], especially the interference of other metal ions in the easily affected samples. It is important to develop a simple and visual photoluminescence (PL) sensing of Fe3+.

In this work, polyamide which was prepared from 1,4-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid and diamine monomers containing adamantane group (PA-CA) have been specially synthesized. The cage structure of adamantane provides enough space for electron transmission in the electrochemical process and has a positive impact on electrochemical stability. PA-CA with high fluorescence quantum efficiency which has selective responsiveness to Fe3+ are expected to be used in the sensing of Fe3+. Furthermore, patterned customized EC devices can display different contents, transmitting decoding information can also be achieved by changing the light and shade of the field. High performance PA-CA shows great potential in corresponding scenarios.

Diamine monomer with amide bond bridging adamantane group and TAA group (1AATAA) and its corresponding polyamide (PA-CA) were prepared according to Scheme 1 and Scheme 2. The specific synthetic routes, materials, instruments and measurements, and characterization were shown in Supporting Information.

1AATAA diamine monomers with adamantane and TPA groups bridged by amide bonds were successfully prepared. The molecular structure of the target monomer was further determined by FTIR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, C-H HSQC and ESI-MS techniques, (the detailed data were in shown in Supporting Information). The diamine monomer exhibits two N-H characteristic stretching bands at 3206 and 3312 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum ( Fig. 1d). FTIR spectrum of PA-CA shows the characteristic stretching of the amide bond at 3294 and 3421 cm−1 (N-H), 1655 cm−1 (CO), 1248 cm−1 (C-N). Notability, the N-H stretching vibration of the amino group completely disappeared in the infrared spectrum of PA-CA, which proves that PA-CA is successfully synthesized.

The thermal stability and solubility of PA-CA were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). PA-CA which contains a distorted conformational TAA skeleton [26] and a large volume of the sterically hindered adamantine group is amorphous and shows a wide peak in the 20° range in Fig. 2a. In addition, the dissolution evaluated the solubility behavior of PA-CA at various solvents (10% w/v, shown in Table S1). As expected, the amorphous structure of PA-CA was completely dissolved in a large polar solvent (NMP, DMF, DMSO), not dissolved in a non-polar solvent (Tol). The large steric hindrance of the adamantine group and the twisted structure of the TAA skeleton increased the internal volume of the polymer to prevent chain accumulation and increase the solubility [27]. The temperature at 354, 366, and 426 ℃ have corresponded to 5%, 10% and 20% weight loss of PA-CA, respectively, which can be attributed to the adamantane group and conjugated TAA skeleton endow its excellent thermal stability. The excellent solubility and thermal stability of PA-CA met the actual scenarios of large-area solution processing and high-temperature processing and were expected to be used in EC displays [28].

To investigate the photophysical properties of PA-CA, UV–vis spectra and PL emission spectra of PA-CA solution (1 ×10−5 mol/L in NMP) and solid film of PA-CA were measured. From Fig. 3a and Table 1, PA-CA solution and solid film exhibit strong characteristic absorption peaks at 323 nm and 319 nm, respectively. Absorption peak with higher absorbance is ascribed to the π-π * transition between the aromatic ring and the central nitrogen atom of TAA unit [29]. In Fig. 3a and 3b, the solution and solid film of PA-CA show strong fluorescence emission, the fluorescence color of PA-CA solution, solid film, and the hydrogel which added PA-CA were depicted in CIE1931 coordinates (X, Y). The PL spectra reveal that the maximum emission peaks are around at 465 nm. This is attributed to the adamantine group and cyclohexyl group are both electron-donor groups and the rigidity and large steric resistance of the adamantine group plays a role in suppressing the geometric deformation of the excited state, which could contribute to suppress the non-radiative decay process [30,31]. The frontline orbital theory further explained the reason why PA-CA has excellent luminescent properties. As shown in Fig. 3c, the electron cloud of the model compound is distributed in the TAA unit that occupies the highest molecular orbital (HOMO) and overlaps with the lowest molecular orbital (LUMO) in a large area. The electron cloud distribution on the whole model molecule promotes the formation of local excitation (LE, π-π * ) from the ground state to the excited state. Therefore, the high overlap between HOMO and LUMO proved that PA-CA is a favorable luminescent agent. The electron cloud of the model compound was mainly located on the conjugate surface, and the HOMO and LUMO overlap in a large area, resulting in almost no change in the density of the electron cloud during the transition from the ground state to the excited state, and the charge transfer process was weakened. In addition, the large space diamond-like alkyl group inhibits the geometric deformation of the excited state, thereby weakening the photon energy loss caused by non-radiative transitions, resulting in high fluorescence quantum efficiency (ΦPL1: solution=46.1%, ΦPL2: film=4.5%) [32]. And the radiation transition constant (K
r ) and non-radiation constant (K
nr ) in NMP solution were calculated by formula Φ
PL1
= τ
PL
× K
r
= K
r
/ (K
r
+ K
nr
). The results obtained are K
r = 0.0900, K
nr = 0.1052, respectively. (τ
PL =5.12 ns, shown in Fig. S6.) [9]. The fluorescence quantum efficiency of flim is lower than solution, which is attributed to the multilayer stacking of solid molecules and the π - π interaction between aromatic rings promoting non radiative relaxation processes, leading to a decrease in fluorescence yield. According to a previous report [33], the hydrogel prepared by mixing NMP solution of PA-CA and the aqueous solution of acrylamide for photoinitiation polymerization reveals a fluorescence emission behavior similar to that of PA-CA solid film, with strong fluorescence emission (ΦPL3: hydrogel =9.3%). In contrast, the blank acrylamide hydrogel does not emit fluorescence under UV excitation. The addition of PA-CA changes fluorescence emission behavior without significantly changing the optical transmittance, which is still colorless and transparent. This is due to the fact that the weak CT between diamine and dianhydride units. Cyclohexane unit of alicyclic group is a weak electron withdrawing group with non-conjugated structure, adamantine group and amide bond are also non-conjugated structures, which reduce the ability of TAA unit to supply electrons. The combined effect of above groups weakens the generation of intramolecular and intermolecular charge transfer complexes (CTC), resulting in a high transmittance (more than 90%) of PA-CA solid film at 400 nm (shown in Fig. 3d) and the hydrogel remains transparent after adding PA-CA [34,35].

The excellent PL emission of PA-CA (NMP solution, 10 μM) was investigated for the selective PL response to Fe3+ , which the response behavior of PA-CA was shown by the change of PL intensity when adding NMP solution of Fe3+ (the purpose is to avoid aggregation-caused quenching phenomenon). The morphology of PA-CA was characterized by TEM and SEM (shown in Fig. S7). From the TEM and SEM images of Figs. S6a and 6b, the particle distribution of PA-CA is relatively uniform, and the particle size is around 120–150 nm. C, N, and O elements simultaneously exist in PA-CA and are evenly distributed on the surface of the material (shown in Fig. S7c). When detecting the sensing performance of PA-CA solution, the uniform particle distribution of PA-CA is helpful for sufficient contact between PA-CA molecules and Fe3+. The PL intensity of PA-CA gradually quenched with increased Fe3+ concentration (the fluorescence quenching percentage reaches 99%) and the position of the emission peaks have no significant change ( Fig. 4a). In order to further understand the process of PA-CA fluorescence quenching before and after adding Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime assays of PA-CA were measured, which is the most effective method to distinguish dynamic quenching from static quenching. As shown in Fig. S5 and S8, after the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime of PA-CA was significantly attenuated, changing from 5.12 ns to 4.73 ns, which indicated that the quenching mechanism had a dynamic quenching effect [36]. Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer plot based on Stern-Volmer equation (I
0
/I=1 +K
sv
[Q]) can be analyzed to better examine the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Fe3+. Excellent linear correlation between quenching rate (I
0
/I) and concentration of Fe3+ was shown in a low concentration range (0–5 μg/mL), linear fitting equation is I
0
/I= 0.9475 + 0.1184[Q
Fe
3+
] (R
2
=0.9993) . The Stern-Volmer quenching constant (K
sv ) calculated by the linear fitting equation is 3.19 × 104 M−1 (shown in Fig. 4b), indicating that PA-CA can quickly respond Fe3+. The comparison of the sensing performance of PA-CA with different fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ was summarized in Table S2 [37–41]. The position of UV absorption peak of PA-CA solution moved significantly with the Fe3+ concentration increase (shown in Fig. 4c). And the correlation between Fe3+ concentration and absorbance were shown in Fig. 4d.

In order to verify the specific response of PA-CA to Fe3+, the ability of PA-CA to respond to Fe3+ when other metal ions coexist with Fe3+ was tested. In Fig. 5a, a single metal ion was added to the PA-CA solution, there was no significant change in the PL intensity of PA-CA, but the PL intensity of PA-CA decreased significantly when Fe3+ were added. The comparative image when a single metal ion was added to the PA-CA solution was shown in Fig. 5b. When multiple ions were added to the PA-CA solution, the PL intensity of PA-CA was decreased slightly (shown in Fig. 5c). After Fe3+ was added to the mixed solution, the PL intensity of the mixed solution was quenched, which indicated that the specific responsiveness of PA-CA to Fe3+. The fluorescence image of PA-CA when other metal ions coexist with Fe3+ were shown in Fig. 5d.

PA-CA has a specific response to Fe3+, which can effectively quench the fluorescence of PA-CA. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism has been investigated. In Fig. 6a, The UV–vis absorption peak of Fe3+ and the excitation spectrum of PA-CA are 309/359 nm and 365 nm respectively. Excitation spectrum of PA-CA overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ in a large area, which has a negative impact on the PL emission of PA-CA [42,43]. This means that the excited light energy of PA-CA will be partially absorbed by Fe3+, which will weaken the PL intensity of PA-CA. Other metal ions have little effect on the fluorescence of PA-CA, and there is no obvious UV–vis absorption near the excitation spectrum of PA-CA (shown in Fig. S9). As the concentration of Fe3+increases, the internal filtration effect (IFE) between Fe3+and PA-CA may be a factor in the quenching of fluorescence. As shown as in Fig. 6b, by comparing the 1HNMR and infrared spectra of PA-CA before and after the addition of Fe3+, it was found that the proton signal on the amide bond of PA-CA shifted by 25.6 Hz and the line width widened after the addition of Fe3+, which indicates that PA-CA has a coordination effect with Fe3+. In the infrared spectrum, the characteristic vibration peak of N-H shifts to high wavenumber and the vibration intensity decreases after the addition of Fe3+ (in Fig. 6b and 6c). This further demonstrates the coordination effect between Fe3+ and PA-CA [36,44,45]. As a result, the fluorescence of PA-CA was quenched by Fe3+, which may be attributed to the synergistic process of IFE effect and coordination, the possible coordination mechanism is shown in Fig. 6d. PA-CA and acrylamide-based hydrogel were combined to construct a fluorescent hydrogel with strong fluorescence emission. Test was carried out with Fe3+ aqueous solution to avoid hydrogel denatured and harden in organic solvent, and the hydrogel was immersed in deionized water for 1 min to eliminate the influence of water on fluorescence quenching. Fluorescence of hydrogel was quenched with the increase of Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence quenching percentage over 98% (Fig. S10), which can be used as a metal probe due to sensitive response of PA-CA to Fe3+.

Cyclic voltammograms (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) of PA-CA were measured in 0.2 M TBAP/ACN solution. As shown in Fig. 7a, PA-CA displays a pair of reversible redox peaks at 0.97 V (oxidation peak) and 0.55 V (reduction peak). An oxidation peak at 0.73 V in the DPV curve corresponds to an electroactive nitrogen center and responds to a color change in PA-CA film, indicating that no significant electron coupling could be observed (shown in Fig. 7b). Thus, this is attributed to the N atom of the electroactive center of the TAA unit, which forms TAA+ during the oxidation process. It was found that the oxidation peak potential shifted positive and the reduction peak potential shifted negative with the scanning rate increase, which proved the quasi reversibility of PA-CA (shown in Fig. 7c). The relationship between peak current (i
p ) and different scanning rates was shown in Fig. 7d. And the nyquist diagrams of PA-CA film was shown in Fig. S11. The peak current increases gradually with the scanning rate increase. An excellent linear relationship between square root of scanning rate (v
1/2 ) and i
p , which indicates this is a diffusion-controlled electrode process with faster counter ion diffusion and the diffusion coefficient was calculated by i
p
=kn
3/2
AD
1/2
Cv
1/2 , the result is 4.12 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 [46].

PA-CA film exhibits prominent oxidation and reduction areas and color of oxidation state that are strongly contrasted with the neutral state in CV test, indicating the redox reaction has a good reversible cyclic property. Therefore, spectroelectrochemical characteristics of PA-CA film were investigated by UV–vis spectrophotometer at the application of increasing potentials to demonstrate its optical transmittance and electrochemical stability. PA-CA film shows a strong absorption peak in the UV region due to the transition of π–π * in the neutral state (applied potential is 0.0 V). PA-CA has no obvious absorption peaks in the spectrum higher than 400 nm before the applied voltage reach 0.4 V (shown as Fig. 8a). With the applied voltage increase, a strong absorption peak emerges at 835 nm and a weak absorption peak at 615 nm and 412 nm. When the applied voltage increases to 1.0 V, the absorption intensity will not alter, representing the complete oxidation of the TAA unit to TAA+ accompanied by the color of the oxidation state that is strongly contrasted with the neutral state. The transmittance of PA-CA film in neutral state and oxidized state were tested respectively. PA-CA film showed a significant change from colorless to blue in CIE coordinates (shown in Fig. 8b). EC behavior and stability of PA-CA film was further demonstrated, transmission change of PA-CA film was monitored in situ by applying 0.0–0.97 V voltage in a dynamic cycle. And the changes of the surface morphology of the PA-CA film during the electrochemical process were further investigated by SEM and TEM. PA-CA film exhibits an excellent cyclic stability and high transmittance (∆T = 70%, Fig. 8c). Transmittance still reaches 80% of the maximum transmittance after more than 10,000 s of circulation. The coloring (tc) and bleaching time (tb) are 4.27 s and 2.86 s, respectively, which are calculated based on reaching 90% of the full-transmittance change (Fig. 8d and Table 2). The PA-CA film is about 600 nm thick and exhibits a smooth surface. During the electrochemical process, the edge stacking of the film becomes loose [46], which facilitates the transport of electrons (shown in Fig. S12). It shows the fast electrochemical response characteristics of PA-CA. Moreover, EC property of PA-CA was examined by coloration efficiency (CE). CE of PA-CA is 239 cm2/C, which is calculated by the formula: CE= ΔOD/Q
d
and ΔOD=log (T
b
/T
c
) . High CE and fast switching response indicates that PA-CA has the potential to be used as high-performance EC devices [47], and the electrochemical properties of PA-CA compared with other types of EC polyamides were summarized in Table S4 [21,47–50].

EFC properties of PA-CA were shown in Fig. 9a. From 0.0–1.0 V, the PL intensity of the PA-CA film decreased gradually increased. The PL emission peak does not shift during the voltage increases, indicating no extra oxidized derivatives are generated during the oxidation process. High fluorescence quenching efficiency can be attributed to the efficient quenching effect of TAA+ [51]. This phenomenon displays the PL intensity can be controlled, and applied in dark field, which due to the effect of TAA transfer to TAA+. In Fig. 9b, encrypted information transmission is particularly important in the era of convenient access to information. EFC characteristic endows PA-CA with potential application value in the field of information encryption. It is supposed that the PA-CA film was coated on a transparent conductive substrate (ITO glass or PET), which was almost colorless and transparent in the visible light range. The information that needs to be encrypted was integrated on the film through pattern customization, and the film can display light and dark changes when a voltage was applied under ultraviolet light, and the customized pattern can be displayed to achieve the purpose of transmitting information.

The single-layer electrochromic device with PA-CA as the electrochromic active layer was assembled to investigative the practical application (in Fig. 10a) [52]. The NMP solution of PA-CA (0.3 mL, 10 μM) was uniformly dripped on the ITO glass, and vacuum-dried to obtain a colorless and transparent PA-CA film, and the letters "XMU" were integrated on the PA-CA film. Afterward, UV curable gel was applied around the electrochromic active layer of device to leave a small hole for injection in electrolyte (0.2 M LiClO4/ACN) and the electrochromic device was a sandwich structure (active area: 2 cm × 2 cm) and colorless in the visible light range, and the patterned letters were difficult to be observed. As shown in Fig. 10b, the device exhibited a significant color change so that the letters were observed when a voltage of 0.0–3.5 V was applied to both ends of the device and two pairs of redox peaks have emerged in the CV curve, the oxidation and two reduction peaks appeared at 3.15 V and 1.21/- 2.89 V, respectively. The device shows strong color contrast and purpose of multi-display can be achieved by writing on the film or patterning customization. CV curve of multiple cycles and the change of device transmissivity were tested to prove the stability for EC device. The intensity of redox peak gradually weakens and the closed area of redox curve continuously decreases during redox test, which is due to the poor performance of working device and decreasing contact area between PA-CA and electrolyte after the continuous electrode process. Above disadvantages can be changed by the optimization of the device structure and electrolyte types. In Fig. 10c and 10d, the transmissivity of device can reach 63% of the maximum transmissivity after 2000 s. In short, the device exhibited a strong color contrast and excellent cycle stability from the neutral state to the oxidation state, which was expected to be used in electrochromic display devices with high contrast and graphic display.

In summary, polyamide containing adamantane group was successfully synthesized, which showed excellent fluorescence quantum efficiency, electrochemical stability and thermal stability. Adamantine alkyl group which is a unique cage like three-dimensional structure has a positive impact on the improvement of the performance of polyamide. PA-CA was used as a probe to achieve specific response to Fe3+, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was proposed due to coordination and IFE. The display of information content of EC device was realized through patterned customization and the electrochemical cycle stability of more than 10,000 s was expected to be applied to a wider display field. PA-CA can be used to encrypt and decode information by controlling the fluorescence intensity of PA-CA film, achieving the change of light and shade.

Dongxu Li: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiaohong Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Juguo Dai: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hao Liu: Investigation. Conghui Yuan: Software. Guorong Chen: Methodology. Yiting Xu: Modify the manuscript. Lizong Dai: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20149, 52273275), Collaborative Innovation Center of Petrochemical Downstream Raw Materials and New Materials Industry, and Xiamen Public Technology Service Platform Oriented Project (YDZX20193502000004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solution-processable neutral green electrochromic polymer containing thieno[3,2-b]thiophene derivative as unconventional donor units
Macromolecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, Y. Guo, J. Shi, H. Yu, H. Meng, Solution-processable neutral green electrochromic polymer containing thieno[3,2-b]thiophene derivative as unconventional donor units, Macromolecules. 49 (2016) 7211–7219. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.macromol.6b01624.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorless to all-black full-NIR high-contrast switching in solid electrochromic films prepared with organic mixed valence systems based on dibenzofulvene derivatives

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of high-performance electrochromic material for facile fabrication of truly black electrochromic device
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.-W. Li, T.-C. Yen, G.-S. Liou. Synthesis of high-performance electrochromic material for facile fabrication of truly black electrochromic device, Electrochim. Acta. 367 (2021) 137474. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2020.137474.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dongxu Li: A doctoral candidate in the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University, focusing on the performance research of new photoelectric active polymers.
Xiaohong Wang: A doctoral candidate in the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University, focusing on electrochemical catalysis and energy.
Juguo Dai: A doctoral candidate in the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University, supercapacitors and energy storage.
Hao Liu: A doctoral student at the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University, focusing on anti-corrosion coating materials.

Yiting Xu* : Professor at the School of Materials, Xiamen University, focusing on the functionalization of functional polymer materials, conductive polymer composites and their applications in anti-corrosion, catalysis, and supercapacitor materials, controllable construction of polymer/inorganic hybrid nanomaterials and supramolecular Assembly, functional coating materials and fire-resistant and flame-retardant materials.
Conghui Yuan: Professor at the School of Materials, Xiamen University, focusing on intelligent polymers, supramolecular driving force regulation, electrochemistry of hydrogel systems.

Guorong Chen: Engineer at the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University, focusing on the design, synthesis and self-assembly of polymers, flame retardant and functional modification of polymer.

Lizong Dai* : Distinguished professor at the School of Materials, Xiamen University, focusing on the synthesis of conductive polymers and electroactive polymers, organic/inorganic internal hybrid polymers and high-performance coating materials, fire-resistant and flame-retardant materials, solid surface coating materials, solid surface controlled construction and functionalization of surface polymers.
